justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch pipeline with feature engineering, model training, validation) and serving (Prediction Service with Redis feature store, in-process inference, async logging) pipelines are thoroughly designed. 100ms P99 constraint is deeply addressed — pre-computed feature store, in-process inference over external model serving, async logging, and model complexity guidance (gradient-boosted trees over deep learning). Feature store identified as necessary with detailed rationale. Model versioning with blue-green deployment and validation gate provides rollback. Team size factored throughout — explicitly avoids Kubeflow/MLflow/Seldon, recommends simple tools (S3, Redis, Postgres, cron). Trade-offs and risks are extensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptionally detailed hot-path vs cold-path separation with concrete latency budgets (80ms client timeout, <5ms Redis, <5ms inference)

Concept drift and data pipeline failure risks identified with concrete mitigations

trials.justification: All rubric criteria met: Both training (weekly batch) and serving (Prediction Service) pipelines are thoroughly designed. 100ms P99 constraint is explicitly addressed with a latency budget breakdown (network ~20ms, feature lookup ~10ms, inference ~5ms, serialization ~5ms, headroom ~60ms) and model complexity guidance (LightGBM/XGBoost over deep learning). Feature store identified as essential with online (Redis) and batch (Parquet) stores. Model versioning with blue-green deployment and canary rollout provides rollback. Team size factored throughout — in-process serving over TF Serving, managed services recommended, explicit warnings against self-hosting MLflow/Kubeflow. Trade-offs and risks are extensive and well-reasoned.

observations.text: Exceptional latency budget breakdown showing exactly how 100ms P99 is achieved across network, feature lookup, inference, and serialization
Feature store design covers both online (Redis) and batch (Parquet) with staleness detection and fallback to local cache
Model versioning includes three states (candidate, canary, production, retired) with blue-green deployment and auto-rollback on MAE degradation >15%
Team size constraint deeply integrated — in-process model serving eliminates network hop, managed services recommended, explicit warnings against self-hosting ML platforms
Phased implementation plan is practical: start with heuristic in weeks 1-3, add ML model in weeks 4-6, add canary/monitoring in weeks 7-8
Training-serving skew identified as critical risk with concrete mitigation (shared transformation code, integration test asserting equality)
Graceful degradation chain: Redis → local cache → rule-based heuristic, ensuring checkout never fails
Data drift risk addressed with monitoring (KL divergence/PSI) and ability to trigger ad-hoc retraining

observations.text: Exceptional detail on the hot path latency budget breakdown proving 100ms P99 is achievable
Shadow Evaluator component adds post-deployment safety beyond basic rollback
Training-serving skew identified as critical risk with concrete mitigation (single feature definition, integration tests)
Phased implementation guidance is practical and well-sequenced for a 4-person team
Explicit pitfalls-to-avoid section directly addresses common ML system failures
Feature store staleness risk called out with per-feature refresh intervals
Fallback strategy for Redis failure (static lookup table) shows resilience thinking
In-process model serving rationale is well-argued for team size constraint
